hook.text: A coin lands heads 8 out of 10 times. Is it rigged, or did you just get lucky?

buildup.text: A hypothesis test answers this formally. It asks: could the result happen by random chance alone?

discovery.text: You start by assuming nothing special is happening — the null hypothesis. Then you check if data contradicts it.

twist.text: You never prove the alternative. You only reject or fail to reject the null. Absence of evidence isn't evidence.

climax.text: Every drug trial, psychology study, and A/B test uses this logic. It's the backbone of modern science.

punchline.text: Assume nothing, then let the data disagree.

quiz.question: What does a hypothesis test do?
options: It checks whether observed data is unlikely under the assumption that nothing special happened
It proves that a treatment works
It calculates the exact probability of a theory being true

hook.text: In court, the defendant is presumed innocent. In statistics, the null hypothesis is the presumed truth.

buildup.text: The null says there's no effect, no difference, no relationship. It's the boring default explanation.

discovery.text: You design the test to look for evidence against the null. If the data is extreme enough, you reject it.

twist.text: Failing to reject the null doesn't mean it's true. It means you didn't find enough evidence against it.

climax.text: The null hypothesis is a straw man built to be knocked down — but only by strong enough evidence.

punchline.text: Start with 'nothing happened' and see if data disagrees.

quiz.question: What does failing to reject the null hypothesis mean?
options: There wasn't enough evidence to disprove it — not that it's true
The null hypothesis is definitely correct
The experiment failed and must be repeated

hook.text: A drug company believes their pill reduces blood pressure. That's the alternative hypothesis — the claim to test.

buildup.text: The alternative is what you suspect is true. It's the exciting possibility that something real is going on.

discovery.text: One-sided alternatives predict a direction: 'the drug lowers pressure.' Two-sided: 'the drug changes pressure.'

twist.text: Fisher picked 0.05 arbitrarily. Yet it became a sacred threshold that entire careers hinge on.

climax.text: Science advances by elimination, not confirmation. You rule out the boring to reveal the interesting.

punchline.text: You don't prove the alternative. You disprove the null.

quiz.question: How does a hypothesis test evaluate the alternative hypothesis?
options: Indirectly — by checking if the null hypothesis is unlikely enough to reject
By directly calculating the probability the alternative is true
By comparing the alternative to all other possibilities

hook.text: The p-value is the most used and most misunderstood number in all of science.

buildup.text: It answers one question: if the null is true, how likely is data this extreme or more extreme?

discovery.text: A p-value of 0.03 means there's a 3% chance of seeing data this extreme if nothing is really happening.

twist.text: The p-value is NOT the probability the hypothesis is true. That's the most common misinterpretation in science.

climax.text: Small p-value: the data is surprising under the null. That's it. No more, no less.

punchline.text: P-values measure surprise, not truth.

quiz.question: What does a p-value of 0.03 actually mean?
options: If the null hypothesis were true, there's a 3% chance of seeing data this extreme
There's a 3% chance the null hypothesis is true
The treatment works 97% of the time

hook.text: If p < 0.05, we call it 'statistically significant.' But who picked 0.05, and why?

buildup.text: Ronald Fisher suggested 0.05 in 1925 as a convenient threshold — roughly 1 in 20 odds.

discovery.text: It stuck. Journals publish studies with p < 0.05 and reject those above. The threshold became a gatekeeper.

twist.text: 0.05 is arbitrary. A p-value of 0.049 isn't meaningfully different from 0.051, but one is 'significant.'

climax.text: The bright line between significant and not is a human invention, not a mathematical necessity.

punchline.text: 0.05 is a convention, not a commandment.

quiz.question: Why is the 0.05 threshold controversial?
options: It's arbitrary — a p-value of 0.049 isn't meaningfully different from 0.051
It's too strict for most experiments
It was chosen by a computer algorithm

hook.text: You know the population average and standard deviation. A sample comes in. Is it different?

buildup.text: The z-test compares a sample mean to a known population mean using the normal distribution.

discovery.text: Convert the difference to a z-score. More than 2 standard deviations away? That's suspicious.

twist.text: The z-test requires knowing the population standard deviation — which is almost never available in practice.

climax.text: It's the simplest test and the foundation for all others. Most real-world tests are variations on it.

punchline.text: How far from expected? The z-score measures that.

quiz.question: When is a z-test appropriate?
options: When the population standard deviation is known and you want to compare a sample mean to it
When the sample size is very small
When comparing two unknown populations

hook.text: A new teaching method is tried on 25 students. Their scores are higher. Is it real or random noise?

buildup.text: The t-test handles small samples where the population standard deviation is unknown.

discovery.text: It uses the sample's own standard deviation and adds extra uncertainty through wider tails.

twist.text: William Gosset invented it at Guinness Brewery. His employer banned publishing, so he used the name 'Student.'

climax.text: The t-test is the workhorse of science. Most published results comparing two groups rely on it.

punchline.text: Small sample? Unknown variance? Use the t-test.

quiz.question: What makes the t-test different from the z-test?
options: It uses the sample's own standard deviation and accounts for extra uncertainty in small samples
It only works with large samples
It doesn't require any assumptions about the data

hook.text: A die is rolled 600 times. Each face should appear 100 times. But some faces show up more. Is it rigged?

buildup.text: The chi-squared test compares observed counts to expected counts across categories.

discovery.text: It sums the squared differences between observed and expected, scaled by the expected count.

twist.text: It tests association, not causation. Two variables being linked in the table doesn't mean one causes the other.

climax.text: Genetics, marketing, and election analysis all use chi-squared to test if categorical patterns are real.

punchline.text: Do the counts match expectations? Chi-squared knows.

quiz.question: What type of data does the chi-squared test analyze?
options: Categorical counts — comparing observed frequencies to expected frequencies
Continuous measurements like height or weight
Time series data over many years

hook.text: Three diets are tested. Diet A loses 5 kg, B loses 7 kg, C loses 6 kg. Is the difference real?

buildup.text: ANOVA — analysis of variance — compares means across three or more groups simultaneously.

discovery.text: It splits total variation into 'between groups' and 'within groups.' If between dominates, the groups differ.

twist.text: ANOVA tells you groups differ but not which ones. You need follow-up tests to find the specific differences.

climax.text: Agricultural science invented ANOVA. Today it's used in medicine, psychology, and every field testing multiple options.

punchline.text: Comparing three or more? Don't use multiple t-tests. Use ANOVA.

quiz.question: What does ANOVA tell you?
options: Whether there's a significant difference among three or more group means
Exactly which groups are different from each other
The correlation between two variables

hook.text: What if your data isn't normally distributed? Most tests assume it is. Non-parametric tests don't.

buildup.text: They work with ranks instead of raw values. Convert data to 1st, 2nd, 3rd and compare the rankings.

discovery.text: The Mann-Whitney test replaces the t-test. The Kruskal-Wallis replaces ANOVA. Same logic, fewer assumptions.

twist.text: They're safer but less powerful. With less assumed, you need more data to detect the same effect.

climax.text: When your data is messy, skewed, or ordinal, non-parametric tests are the honest choice.

punchline.text: Fewer assumptions, wider applicability, less power. A fair trade.

quiz.question: When should you use non-parametric tests?
options: When your data violates the normality assumption or is ordinal/ranked
Only when sample sizes are very large
When you want the most powerful test available

hook.text: A fire alarm goes off but there's no fire. You evacuated for nothing. That's a false positive.

buildup.text: A Type I error means rejecting the null hypothesis when it's actually true. You see a pattern that isn't real.

discovery.text: The significance level — usually 0.05 — is the maximum Type I error rate you're willing to accept.

twist.text: If you run 20 tests at 0.05, you expect one false positive by chance. Multiple testing multiplies the risk.

climax.text: False positives fill journals with results that can't be replicated. They're the engine of bad science.

punchline.text: Seeing a pattern that isn't there. That's Type I.

quiz.question: What is a Type I error?
options: Rejecting the null hypothesis when it's actually true — a false positive
Failing to reject the null when it's false — a false negative
Getting a p-value exactly equal to 0.05

hook.text: A cancer screening says you're healthy, but the tumor is there. The test missed it.

buildup.text: A Type II error means failing to reject the null when it's actually false. A real effect goes undetected.

discovery.text: The probability of a Type II error is called beta. The smaller beta is, the more sensitive your test.

twist.text: Type I and Type II errors trade off. Making one harder to commit makes the other easier.

climax.text: In medicine, missing a disease is often worse than a false alarm. The stakes determine which error matters more.

punchline.text: Missing a real effect is the silent error.

quiz.question: What is a Type II error?
options: Failing to reject the null hypothesis when it's actually false — missing a real effect
Rejecting the null hypothesis incorrectly
Getting a sample size that's too large

hook.text: Your study finds no effect. Does that mean the drug doesn't work — or that your study was too small to detect it?

buildup.text: Statistical power is the probability of detecting a real effect. Low power means you'll probably miss it.

discovery.text: Power depends on sample size, effect size, and significance level. Bigger samples and bigger effects boost power.

twist.text: Many published studies have power below 50%. They're coin flips — even real effects get missed half the time.

climax.text: Running an underpowered study is nearly pointless. If power is low, even real effects stay hidden.

punchline.text: If your study can't detect the effect, why run it?

quiz.question: What determines statistical power?
options: Sample size, effect size, and significance level
Only the p-value
The number of hypotheses tested

topics.title: Effect Size: How Big Is the Difference?

hook.text: A drug lowers blood pressure by 0.1 mmHg with p < 0.001. Statistically significant — but medically worthless.

buildup.text: Effect size measures how large a difference is, independent of sample size. P-values can't do that.

discovery.text: Cohen's d, odds ratios, and correlation coefficients all measure effect size in different contexts.

twist.text: With a large enough sample, any tiny difference becomes significant. Significance without effect size is meaningless.

climax.text: Journals increasingly require effect sizes alongside p-values. A significant result must also be meaningful.

punchline.text: Significant isn't the same as important.

quiz.question: Why is effect size important alongside statistical significance?
options: A statistically significant result can be trivially small — effect size shows if it's meaningful
Effect size replaces the need for p-values entirely
It's only needed in medical studies

hook.text: Test 20 random jelly bean colors for causing acne. One will test positive at p < 0.05 by pure chance.

buildup.text: Each test has a 5% false positive rate. Run 20 tests and the overall false positive rate soars to 64%.

discovery.text: The Bonferroni correction divides the threshold by the number of tests: 20 tests means p < 0.0025.

twist.text: Bonferroni is conservative. It cuts false positives but also kills real findings. Better methods exist.

climax.text: Genomics tests millions of genes simultaneously. Without correction, every study would find false discoveries.

punchline.text: Test enough things and something will look real. Correct for it.

quiz.question: What is the multiple comparisons problem?
options: Running many tests inflates the chance of false positives — some will be significant by luck
Having too many participants in a study
Comparing more than two groups with a t-test

hook.text: Most researchers think a p-value is the probability the null is true. It's not. Not even close.

buildup.text: Common mistakes: 'p = 0.03 means 3% chance the null is true.' Wrong. 'p = 0.03 means the effect is real.' Also wrong.

discovery.text: P-values only tell you how surprising the data is under the null. They say nothing about truth or importance.

twist.text: Even textbooks get it wrong. The misunderstanding is so widespread it's practically a collective delusion.

climax.text: Understanding what p-values actually mean is one of the most important statistical literacy skills.

punchline.text: The most used number in science is the most misunderstood.

quiz.question: Which is a correct interpretation of a p-value of 0.03?
options: If the null is true, there's a 3% chance of data this extreme or more extreme
There's a 3% chance the null hypothesis is true
The effect is real with 97% certainty

hook.text: Try enough analyses on the same data and something will hit p < 0.05. It's called p-hacking.

buildup.text: Researchers test multiple variables, drop outliers, and split groups until a significant result appears.

discovery.text: Each decision is defensible alone. Together, they inflate false positives far beyond 5%.

twist.text: Most p-hacking isn't fraud — it's unconscious. Researchers genuinely want to find something and nudge the analysis.

climax.text: Pre-registration — declaring your analysis plan before seeing data — is the main defense against p-hacking.

punchline.text: If you fish long enough, you'll always catch something.

quiz.question: What is p-hacking?
options: Trying multiple analyses until a statistically significant result appears
Deliberately fabricating data
Using a p-value threshold of 0.10 instead of 0.05

hook.text: A study finds that left-handed people earn more. The researcher writes: 'We predicted left-handers earn more.'

buildup.text: HARKing means forming your hypothesis after seeing the results, then pretending you predicted it all along.

discovery.text: Researchers test many hypotheses, then present the significant one as if it was planned all along.

twist.text: HARKing is invisible to readers. Published papers rarely admit which hypotheses came after the data.

climax.text: Registered reports require hypotheses before data collection. This makes HARKing structurally impossible.

punchline.text: Predictions made after the fact aren't predictions.

quiz.question: What is HARKing?
options: Hypothesizing after results are known — pretending you predicted what the data showed
Making predictions before collecting data
Running too many statistical tests

hook.text: In 2016, the American Statistical Association did something unprecedented: it warned scientists about p-values.

buildup.text: The ASA released six principles clarifying what p-values can and cannot do. It was a landmark rebuke.

discovery.text: Key points: p-values don't measure effect probability, aren't evidence quality, and 0.05 isn't a bright line.

twist.text: Despite the statement, most journals still require p < 0.05 for publication. Change is painfully slow.

climax.text: The ASA acknowledged what statisticians knew for decades: p-values are overused, abused, and misunderstood.

punchline.text: Even statisticians say we overrely on p-values.

quiz.question: What did the 2016 ASA statement warn about?
options: P-values are widely misunderstood, overused, and shouldn't be treated as a bright-line rule
P-values should be replaced by Bayesian methods
The 0.05 threshold should be lowered to 0.01

hook.text: If p-values are so flawed, what should scientists use instead? The debate is far from settled.

buildup.text: Effect sizes show how big the result is. Confidence intervals show the range of plausible values.

discovery.text: Bayesian methods calculate the actual probability of a hypothesis, given the data — what people think p-values do.

twist.text: No single metric is perfect. Effect sizes need context. Bayes requires choosing a prior. Each has trade-offs.

climax.text: The future is likely a toolkit approach: p-values plus effect sizes plus intervals, never one number alone.

punchline.text: No single number captures truth. Use many.

quiz.question: What advantage do Bayesian methods have over p-values?
options: They calculate the probability of a hypothesis given the data
They require no assumptions
They always give smaller p-values

hook.text: The average height is 170 cm, plus or minus 3 cm. That 'plus or minus' is a confidence interval.

buildup.text: A confidence interval gives a range of plausible values for a parameter, not just a single point estimate.

discovery.text: A 95% CI means: if you repeated this study 100 times, about 95 intervals would contain the true value.

twist.text: It does NOT mean there's a 95% probability the true value is inside this specific interval. Subtle but critical.

climax.text: Confidence intervals convey both the estimate and its uncertainty. A single number hides the doubt.

punchline.text: A range honest about uncertainty beats a precise lie.

quiz.question: What does a 95% confidence interval actually tell you?
options: If you repeated the study many times, about 95% of intervals would contain the true value
There's a 95% probability the true value is in this interval
The result is statistically significant at the 5% level

hook.text: The drug reduces pain by 2 to 8 points. Another study says 4.9 to 5.1 points. Which is more informative?

buildup.text: A narrow interval means high precision — the estimate is tightly pinned down. A wide interval means uncertainty.

discovery.text: Width depends on sample size and variability. Bigger samples and less noisy data yield narrower intervals.

twist.text: A narrow interval around a useless effect is still useless. Width tells precision, not importance.

climax.text: Always check both the location and the width of the interval. Together they tell the full story.

punchline.text: Narrow means precise. It doesn't mean important.

quiz.question: What does a wide confidence interval indicate?
options: High uncertainty — the true value could be in a large range
The result is not statistically significant
The effect is clinically important

hook.text: Group A: 50 ± 5. Group B: 55 ± 5. The intervals overlap. Does that mean there's no real difference?

buildup.text: Many people assume overlapping CIs mean no significant difference. This is a common mistake.

discovery.text: CIs can overlap slightly and still show a significant difference. The comparison interval is narrower.

twist.text: Non-overlapping CIs always mean a significant difference. But overlapping CIs don't guarantee insignificance.

climax.text: To properly compare groups, use a confidence interval for the difference — not separate intervals.

punchline.text: Overlap doesn't mean equal. Do the comparison properly.

quiz.question: What is true about overlapping confidence intervals?
options: They don't necessarily mean the difference is insignificant — use the interval for the difference
They always indicate no significant difference
They mean the two groups are identical

hook.text: A p-value says 'significant or not.' A confidence interval says 'how much, and how uncertain.'

buildup.text: If a 95% CI for the difference doesn't include zero, it's significant at the 0.05 level. They're linked.

discovery.text: But the CI also shows the effect size and precision. It's a richer, more informative summary than p alone.

twist.text: You can have a significant p-value with a CI so wide it's practically useless. The p-value hides this.

climax.text: Many statisticians argue we should report CIs instead of p-values. They show everything p-values do and more.

punchline.text: P-values give a verdict. CIs tell the whole story.

quiz.question: What advantage do confidence intervals have over p-values?
options: They show both the effect size and precision, not just whether a result is significant
They're always narrower than p-values
They don't require statistical assumptions

hook.text: A 95% credible interval actually means what everyone thinks a 95% confidence interval means.

buildup.text: Bayesian credible intervals say: there's a 95% probability the parameter is inside this range.

discovery.text: This direct probability statement requires a prior — your belief before seeing data. Frequentists avoid priors.

twist.text: With enough data, credible and confidence intervals converge. The philosophical difference fades with sample size.

climax.text: The credible interval is more intuitive but requires more assumptions. Neither approach is universally better.

punchline.text: It means what you always wished confidence intervals meant.

quiz.question: How does a Bayesian credible interval differ from a confidence interval?
options: It gives a direct probability that the parameter is in the range — but requires a prior
It's always wider than a confidence interval
It doesn't use any data

hook.text: Google once tested 41 shades of blue for a link color. The winning shade earned $200 million extra per year.

buildup.text: A/B testing randomly shows users version A or B and measures which performs better.

discovery.text: It's a randomized controlled trial — the gold standard of causal evidence — applied to web pages.

twist.text: Running too many A/B tests simultaneously inflates false positives. Companies run thousands.

climax.text: Every button, headline, and feature you see online was probably tested. A/B testing shapes the modern internet.

punchline.text: The internet you see was chosen by hypothesis tests.

quiz.question: What is A/B testing?
options: A randomized experiment comparing two versions to see which performs better
A survey asking users which design they prefer
A test that only tech companies can use

hook.text: What if researchers had to declare their hypothesis and analysis plan before collecting any data?

buildup.text: Pre-registration locks in your plan publicly before the study begins. No post-hoc changes allowed.

discovery.text: It prevents p-hacking, HARKing, and selective reporting. You test what you said you'd test.

twist.text: Critics say it kills exploration. Supporters say exploration is fine — just label it honestly as exploratory.

climax.text: Major journals now offer 'registered reports' — accepting papers before results are known. Method matters, not outcomes.

punchline.text: Commit to the plan before you see the data.

quiz.question: What is the purpose of pre-registration?
options: To prevent post-hoc changes to hypotheses and analysis plans that inflate false positives
To make studies more expensive
To limit the number of researchers who can study a topic

hook.text: Do you predict the drug will help, or just that it will have some effect — good or bad?

buildup.text: A one-tailed test looks for an effect in one direction only. A two-tailed test looks in both directions.

discovery.text: One-tailed tests are more powerful in the predicted direction but miss effects in the opposite direction.

twist.text: Researchers sometimes switch to one-tailed after seeing results, halving their p-value. This is cheating.

climax.text: Most fields default to two-tailed tests. One-tailed requires strong justification before data collection.

punchline.text: Pick your direction before you see the data. Or test both.

quiz.question: When is a one-tailed test appropriate?
options: When there's strong prior reason to predict the direction of an effect — decided before data collection
Whenever you want a smaller p-value
Only for medical studies

hook.text: A study of 10 million finds that coffee drinkers live 0.3 days longer. p < 0.00001. Should you care?

buildup.text: Statistical significance means the effect is unlikely due to chance. Practical significance means the effect matters.

discovery.text: With huge samples, trivially small effects become statistically significant. Big data amplifies tiny differences.

twist.text: A 0.3-day lifespan gain won't change any health recommendation. But the p-value screams 'significant!'

climax.text: Always ask two questions: is it real, and does it matter? Statistics answers the first. Judgment answers the second.

punchline.text: Real and meaningful are two different things.

quiz.question: What's the difference between statistical and practical significance?
options: Statistical significance means the effect is real; practical significance means it's large enough to matter
They mean the same thing
Practical significance only applies to business

hook.text: The t-test says significant. The Mann-Whitney says not. The Bayesian analysis is ambiguous. Now what?

buildup.text: Different tests make different assumptions. When those assumptions hold differently, results diverge.

discovery.text: Check the assumptions of each test: normality, equal variances, sample size. The test whose assumptions hold wins.

twist.text: Sometimes no test clearly wins. The honest answer is 'the evidence is ambiguous' — but journals rarely publish that.

climax.text: Disagreeing tests are informative. They reveal that the conclusion depends on assumptions, not just data.

punchline.text: When tests disagree, the assumptions are talking.

quiz.question: What should you do when different statistical tests give different results?
options: Check which test's assumptions best match your data — the answer depends on the assumptions
Always trust the test with the lowest p-value
Run more tests until they agree
